events.text: The patient is a 55-year-old female named Renata Okonkwo. She is of West African and Irish descent, with a fair-to-medium complexion. She is a non-smoker and works as a landscape architect. She has no significant family history of melanoma but has a history of multiple atypical nevi since her 30s. She has a past medical history of hypothyroidism (on levothyroxine) and a remote episode of anterior uveitis.

At age 55 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage IV (AJCC 8th edition, M1c) MELANOMA, MALIGNANT (ICD-O histology 8720/3), primary site coded as SKIN OF TRUNK (ICD-O C44.5), arising from a dysplastic nevus on the mid-back. TNM staging: T4b N3c M1c. Summary stage: Distant. Relevant biomarkers included BRAF V600E mutation: POSITIVE; PD-L1 TPS: 15%; LDH: 312 U/L (elevated, 1.5x ULN). Site-specific data elements: Clark Level V, Breslow thickness 5.8 mm, ulceration: PRESENT, mitotic rate: 8/mm2, microsatellite instability: STABLE, tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes: BRISK. Metastatic sites at presentation: multiple cutaneous/subcutaneous nodules on trunk and upper extremities (largest 3.1 cm), bilateral axillary and inguinal lymphadenopathy (largest 4.2 cm right axillary node), and multiple hepatic metastases (up to 2.4 cm). Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY via excisional biopsy of primary and core needle biopsy of right axillary node.

At age 55, the patient had a pathology result of type ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY from excisional biopsy of the primary lesion (mid-back skin). Gross description: 2.5 x 2.0 cm irregularly pigmented lesion with satellite nodule. Microscopic: Malignant melanoma, nodular type, arising from a compound dysplastic nevus. Breslow thickness 5.8 mm, Clark level V. Ulceration present. Mitotic rate 8 per mm2. Perineural invasion: absent. Lymphovascular invasion: present. Deep and peripheral margins: positive at the deep margin. Immunohistochemistry: SOX10 positive, S100 positive, HMB-45 positive, Melan-A positive, Ki-67 approximately 45%. Sentinel lymph node biopsy: right axillary nodes, 3 of 4 positive for metastatic melanoma (largest deposit 4.2 cm with extranodal extension). Concurrent core needle biopsy of a subcutaneous abdominal wall nodule also confirmed metastatic melanoma, identical immunophenotype.

At age 55, the patient had a PET-CT (whole body with contrast-enhanced CT of chest/abdomen/pelvis) at time of staging workup. Findings: Hypermetabolic primary lesion site post-biopsy changes on dorsal trunk. Multiple bilateral axillary lymph nodes showing intense FDG uptake (largest right axillary 4.2 cm, SUVmax 18.4; left axillary 1.8 cm, SUVmax 9.2). Bilateral inguinal lymphadenopathy (right 2.1 cm, SUVmax 7.6; left 1.4 cm, SUVmax 5.3). Multiple FDG-avid subcutaneous/cutaneous nodules scattered over the trunk and bilateral upper extremities (up to 3.1 cm, SUVmax 14.2). Hepatic metastases: 6 discrete lesions throughout both lobes (largest segment 6 lesion 2.4 cm, SUVmax 11.8; additional lesions 0.8-1.7 cm). No pulmonary parenchymal disease. No brain metastases on concurrent MRI brain (with gadolinium). No osseous metastases. Impression: Widespread metastatic melanoma involving bilateral nodal basins, subcutaneous soft tissues, and liver. Stage IV M1c disease.

At age 55 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for metastatic melanoma based on a specimen obtained from the right axillary lymph node core biopsy. A comprehensive 500-gene panel (Foundation One CDx) was used. Results showed: BRAF p.V600E mutation (allele frequency 48%, COSMIC hotspot, tier 1 actionable); CDKN2A p.Q95* nonsense mutation (allele frequency 52%, loss of function); CDKN2B homozygous deletion (copy number 0); PTEN p.R130G missense mutation (allele frequency 31%, likely pathogenic, loss of function); NF1 splice site mutation c.1392-1G>A (allele frequency 22%, likely pathogenic); TP53 p.R248W missense mutation (allele frequency 39%, pathogenic, gain of function); TERT promoter mutation c.-124C>T (allele frequency 55%); MCL1 amplification (copy number 8); MYC copy number gain (copy number 5); CDK4 copy number gain (copy number 4); RAC1 p.P29S mutation (allele frequency 18%); MAP2K1 wild-type; MEK1 wild-type; NRAS wild-type; KIT wild-type; GNAQ wild-type; GNA11 wild-type. Tumor mutational burden: 28 mutations/Mb (HIGH). Microsatellite status: stable (MSS). PD-L1 by companion diagnostic: 15% TPS. No NTRK fusions detected. No ALK, ROS1 rearrangements. No MET amplification. Germline testing via blood sample: BRCA1/2 wild-type; no pathogenic germline variants identified. Notable co-mutation pattern: BRAF V600E with concurrent PTEN loss and NF1 mutation — a recognized pattern associated with resistance to BRAF/MEK targeted therapy.

At age 55, the patient had an oncologist office assessment at the time of initial diagnosis and staging completion. Cancer present. Performance status: ECOG 1. The patient presented with symptomatic disease including a tender right axillary mass, pruritic cutaneous nodules, and fatigue. LDH mildly elevated at 312 U/L. Given BRAF V600E positivity, excellent performance status, and high disease burden with hepatic involvement, the treatment team recommended initiating BRAF/MEK targeted therapy with dabrafenib plus trametinib for rapid disease control. Enrollment in clinical trial considered but patient declined given desire to start treatment promptly. History of hypothyroidism noted; levothyroxine to be continued. Baseline ophthalmologic exam scheduled given history of uveitis. Baseline liver function tests reviewed: AST 52 U/L, ALT 47 U/L, total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 128 U/L.

At age 55 years, the patient received palliative-intent (disease control/first-line) dabrafenib 150 mg twice daily plus trametinib 2 mg once daily (oral targeted therapy, BRAF/MEK inhibitor combination). Initiated for metastatic melanoma, BRAF V600E positive, Stage IV M1c. Cycle 1 Day 1. Planned indefinitely until progression or intolerance.

At age 55 (approximately 6 weeks after starting dabrafenib/trametinib), the patient developed Grade 2 pyrexia (maximum temperature 39.4 degrees C) consistent with dabrafenib-induced fever syndrome. Associated with rigors, myalgias, and fatigue lasting 3 days. Management: dabrafenib held for 3 days, acetaminophen and oral hydration. Trametinib continued. Naproxen added as prophylaxis. After resolution, dabrafenib restarted at full dose. No hospital admission required. This adverse event was attributed to dabrafenib (CTCAE Grade 2 pyrexia). The event resolved completely.

At age 55 (approximately 8 weeks after starting dabrafenib/trametinib), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast for first restaging assessment. Findings demonstrate marked partial response. Bilateral axillary lymph nodes: right axillary node decreased from 4.2 cm to 1.1 cm (74% reduction); left axillary node decreased from 1.8 cm to 0.6 cm. Bilateral inguinal lymph nodes: right 2.1 cm to 0.6 cm; left 1.4 cm to undetectable. Subcutaneous nodules: largest decreased from 3.1 cm to 0.7 cm; multiple others no longer visible or markedly reduced. Hepatic metastases: largest (segment 6) decreased from 2.4 cm to 0.9 cm; 3 of the smaller lesions no longer detectable; 3 remaining lesions decreased by 55-70%. LDH normalized to 187 U/L. Impression: Marked partial response to dabrafenib/trametinib by RECIST 1.1 criteria (>50% decrease in sum of target lesions). No new lesions. Consistent with rapid early response to BRAF/MEK inhibition.

At age 55 (8 weeks on dabrafenib/trametinib), the patient had an oncologist office assessment. Cancer present, with marked response to therapy. ECOG performance status improved to 0. The patient reports resolution of pain and significant improvement in energy. Cutaneous nodules no longer palpable. Right axillary fullness improved. She experienced one episode of fever (Grade 2, managed at home as above). She was counseled on the expected median duration of response to BRAF/MEK inhibition (approximately 10-14 months in historical data) and the importance of surveillance for progression. The PTEN loss and NF1 co-mutations noted on NGS were discussed as potential mediators of earlier resistance. The plan to transition to immunotherapy upon progression was reviewed in advance. Thyroid function: TSH 2.1 mIU/L (normal on levothyroxine). Continued dabrafenib/trametinib.

At age 55 (approximately 6 months after starting dabrafenib/trametinib), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast for interval restaging. Findings: Further response in most sites. Bilateral axillary lymph nodes: right axillary node now 0.8 cm (borderline); left axillary node 0.4 cm. All inguinal nodes undetectable. Subcutaneous nodules: none clearly identified. Hepatic metastases: 2 residual lesions, largest 0.6 cm, one additional lesion no longer detectable. LDH: 156 U/L (normal). Impression: Best response achieved, meeting criteria for partial response. No new lesions. Ongoing partial response maintained.

At age 55 (approximately 7 months after starting dabrafenib/trametinib), the patient developed Grade 1-2 squamoproliferative skin lesions: two cutaneous squamous cell carcinomas (SCC) on the left forearm and right shin, identified on routine dermatologic surveillance. Managed with simple excision by dermatology; both lesions confirmed well-differentiated SCC with clear margins on pathology. This is a known class effect of BRAF inhibitors (paradoxical MAPK pathway activation in BRAF wild-type cells). Dabrafenib dose held briefly for one week while healing occurred, then restarted. Trametinib continued throughout (MEK inhibition partially mitigates this risk). Patient reminded of ongoing dermatologic surveillance every 8 weeks.

At age 55 (approximately 8 months after starting dabrafenib/trametinib), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast plus MRI brain with gadolinium for urgent restaging prompted by new symptoms (new right upper quadrant discomfort and a new palpable right cervical lymph node). Findings: Evidence of disease progression. New enlarged right level II cervical lymph node, 2.2 cm with central necrosis. Previously responding right axillary lymph node re-enlarged to 2.8 cm. New hepatic lesions: 3 new lesions in the right lobe of liver, ranging 0.8-1.5 cm. Prior residual hepatic lesions enlarged: segment 6 lesion now 1.4 cm (from 0.6 cm). New small peritoneal nodule adjacent to the falciform ligament, 0.9 cm. MRI brain: No intracranial metastases. LDH: 389 U/L (elevated). Impression: Progressive disease by RECIST 1.1 criteria, with new anatomic sites of involvement including cervical nodal, peritoneal, and worsening hepatic disease. No brain metastases. Consistent with acquired resistance to BRAF/MEK inhibition at 8 months.

At age 55, following disease progression on dabrafenib/trametinib, the patient underwent core needle biopsy of the right cervical lymph node (new lesion) for histologic confirmation and resistance profiling. Pathology result: ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY. Histology: metastatic melanoma, morphologically similar to prior biopsy. IHC: SOX10 positive, S100 positive, HMB-45 positive, Ki-67 approximately 55% (increased from prior). Note: A liquid biopsy (circulating tumor DNA, Guardant360) was also sent concurrently. Results revealed persistence of the BRAF V600E mutation (VAF 3.2% in ctDNA); emergence of a MEK1 (MAP2K1) p.C121S mutation (VAF 1.8%), a recognized acquired resistance mutation to MEK inhibition; and amplification of BRAF (detected in ctDNA). This resistance profile is consistent with reactivation of the MAPK pathway, explaining clinical resistance despite ongoing BRAF inhibitor therapy. PTEN loss persisted.

At age 55 (8 months after starting dabrafenib/trametinib), the patient had an oncologist office assessment documenting disease progression. Cancer present. ECOG performance status 1. The patient reports new right-sided neck fullness (confirmed as right cervical lymphadenopathy), worsening fatigue, and intermittent right upper quadrant discomfort. LDH rising at 389 U/L. Liquid biopsy and repeat biopsy results reviewed with patient; acquired MEK1 C121S resistance mutation and BRAF amplification explain the progression. Given prior rapid progression on targeted therapy and emergent resistance, the treatment plan was changed to ipilimumab plus nivolumab combination immunotherapy (CheckMate-067 regimen). Notably, high TMB (28 mut/Mb) and PD-L1 15% favor potential benefit from immunotherapy. Dabrafenib/trametinib discontinued. Patient counseled extensively on immune-related adverse event risk profile, particularly the higher risk with combination immunotherapy. Bowel habits, liver function baseline reviewed. Baseline LFTs: AST 68 U/L, ALT 72 U/L (mildly elevated likely from hepatic mets). Thyroid function normal. Baseline colonoscopy not indicated but stool frequency noted as 1-2x/day baseline.

At age 55 years, the patient received palliative-intent second-line ipilimumab 3 mg/kg plus nivolumab 1 mg/kg intravenously every 3 weeks for 4 cycles (combination immunotherapy, CTLA-4 plus PD-1 blockade), initiated for metastatic melanoma following progression on dabrafenib/trametinib. Cycle 1 Day 1 administered. Plan for 4 cycles of combination followed by nivolumab maintenance (480 mg every 4 weeks) per CheckMate-067 design.

At age 55 (approximately 5 weeks after starting ipilimumab/nivolumab, during cycle 2), the patient developed Grade 1-2 immune-related dermatitis: diffuse maculopapular rash involving the trunk and proximal extremities, approximately 30% body surface area. No mucosal involvement. Managed with topical triamcinolone 0.1% cream and oral diphenhydramine as needed. Both ipilimumab and nivolumab continued at full dose. Rash resolved over 2 weeks with topical steroids. Dermatology co-management initiated.

At age 55 (approximately 9 weeks after starting ipilimumab/nivolumab, 3 days after cycle 3), the patient presented to the emergency department with severe diarrhea (8-10 watery stools per day), crampy abdominal pain, and low-grade fever (38.1 degrees C). Physical exam: diffuse abdominal tenderness, no peritoneal signs. Stool cultures and C. difficile testing negative. Flexible sigmoidoscopy showed diffuse mucosal erythema, edema, and ulceration from the rectum to the sigmoid colon. Biopsy confirmed immune-mediated colitis (lymphocytic and neutrophilic infiltrate, crypt abscesses). Diagnosis: Grade 3 immune-related colitis (CTCAE Grade 3: >7 stools/day over baseline, severe pain, hospitalization required). Management: Ipilimumab and nivolumab both held. IV methylprednisolone 1 mg/kg/day initiated. Patient admitted for IV fluids, bowel rest, and monitoring. After 3 days of IV steroids with partial improvement (stools decreased to 4-5/day), infliximab 5 mg/kg IV administered (steroid-refractory component). Diarrhea resolved to Grade 1 (2-3 stools/day above baseline) within 48 hours of infliximab. Transitioned to oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day with slow taper over 8 weeks. Hospital length of stay: 7 days.

At age 55 (post-hospitalization for Grade 3 immune-related colitis), the patient had an oncologist office assessment approximately 3 weeks after discharge. Cancer status: deferred pending first restaging. The patient has completed the 8-week steroid taper and is now on prednisone 10 mg/day (tapering). Stool frequency back to baseline (1-2/day). No abdominal pain. She completed 3 of planned 4 cycles of ipilimumab/nivolumab prior to the Grade 3 colitis event. Given the severity of colitis, ipilimumab will not be restarted (consistent with standard management of Grade 3 immune colitis). Plan to restart nivolumab monotherapy (480 mg IV every 4 weeks) once prednisone is tapered to below 10 mg/day and colitis is fully resolved. Gastroenterology follow-up arranged. Baseline repeat LFTs prior to resuming nivolumab: AST 38 U/L, ALT 41 U/L (improved from prior elevated levels, suggesting hepatic mets responding). Autoimmune hepatitis not evident. Patient tolerating oral intake well. Weight stable. ECOG performance status 1.

At age 55 (approximately 14 weeks after starting ipilimumab/nivolumab, first restaging after 3 cycles of combination and during steroid taper), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast. Findings: Mixed response with overall favorable trend. Right cervical lymph node: decreased from 2.2 cm to 1.4 cm. Right axillary lymph node: decreased from 2.8 cm to 1.6 cm. Hepatic metastases: variable — largest segment 6 lesion decreased from 1.4 cm to 1.0 cm; one of the new right lobe lesions decreased (1.5 cm to 0.9 cm); one lesion stable (0.8 cm). Peritoneal nodule: stable at 0.9 cm. LDH: 241 U/L (down from 389 U/L at progression). No new lesions. No evidence of immune-related pneumonitis. Bowel wall appearance: mild diffuse colonic wall thickening consistent with resolving colitis (correlate clinically). Impression: Partial response by RECIST 1.1 (approximately 30% reduction in sum of target lesions). No new disease. Favorable early response to immunotherapy.

At age 55 years (approximately 16 weeks after initial start of ipilimumab/nivolumab, after prednisone tapered to below 10 mg and colitis fully resolved), the patient resumed nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks as maintenance immunotherapy for metastatic melanoma. Ipilimumab permanently discontinued due to prior Grade 3 colitis. Patient consented and educated regarding continued immune-related adverse event risk and need to report symptoms promptly.

At age 55 (approximately 6 weeks after resuming nivolumab maintenance, cycle 2), the patient developed Grade 2 immune-related hypothyroidism (TSH 18.4 mIU/L, free T4 0.6 ng/dL). She was already on levothyroxine for pre-existing hypothyroidism; levothyroxine dose was increased from 75 mcg to 112 mcg daily. Symptoms: mild fatigue and cold intolerance. No myxedema. Nivolumab continued without interruption. Endocrinology notified. Thyroid function rechecked at 6 weeks: TSH normalized to 3.2 mIU/L on adjusted levothyroxine dose. Ongoing monitoring every 3 months.

At age 55 (approximately 6 months after starting nivolumab maintenance, approximately 10 months total on immunotherapy), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast plus MRI brain with gadolinium. Findings: Continued and deepening partial response. Right cervical lymph node: decreased from 1.4 cm to 0.6 cm (borderline by size criteria). Right axillary lymph node: decreased from 1.6 cm to 0.7 cm. Hepatic metastases: previously 4 lesions — 2 no longer detectable; remaining 2 lesions decreased to 0.5 cm and 0.4 cm respectively. Peritoneal nodule: no longer visualized. LDH: 162 U/L (normal). MRI brain: no intracranial metastases. No new sites of disease. No evidence of pneumonitis. Colon wall: normal appearance. Impression: Deep partial response by RECIST 1.1 (approximately 65% reduction in sum of target lesions from baseline at start of immunotherapy). No new disease. Durable and deepening response to immunotherapy.

At age 56 (approximately 12 months total on immunotherapy, 8 months on nivolumab maintenance), the patient had an oncologist office assessment. Cancer present but deeply responding. ECOG performance status 0. The patient reports excellent quality of life, has returned to full-time work as a landscape architect, and is maintaining regular physical activity. No active immune-related adverse events. Colitis in full remission (confirmed by gastroenterology). Hypothyroidism well-controlled on adjusted levothyroxine. Skin: no new squamoproliferative lesions or melanoma recurrences. LDH: 154 U/L (normal). Dermatology surveillance: clear on most recent exam 6 weeks prior. Continued nivolumab 480 mg every 4 weeks. Plan for repeat imaging in 3 months. Discussion regarding potential for treatment holiday after 2 years of immunotherapy initiated — patient interested but understands the investigational nature of that approach in M1c disease.

At age 56 (approximately 15 months total on immunotherapy, 11 months on nivolumab maintenance), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast. Findings: Stable to minimal further improvement. Right cervical lymph node: 0.5 cm (borderline, stable). Right axillary lymph node: 0.6 cm (stable). Hepatic lesions: 2 residual lesions, both 0.4 cm and stable (stable). No peritoneal disease. LDH: 148 U/L (normal). No new lesions. No immune-related complications identified. Impression: Stable disease bordering on continued partial response. No progression. Best overall response remains partial response (approximately 67% reduction from immunotherapy baseline). Continued durable benefit from nivolumab monotherapy.

At age 56 (approximately 18 months total on immunotherapy, 14 months on nivolumab maintenance), the patient had an oncologist office assessment. Cancer present (residual partial response, stable). ECOG performance status 0. Stable disease maintained. Discussion of treatment duration: given persistent residual disease on imaging (not achieving complete response), and M1c disease with prior hepatic and peritoneal involvement, the multidisciplinary team recommended continuing nivolumab maintenance. The patient was enrolled in a registry study tracking long-term outcomes on PD-1 maintenance after combination immunotherapy. Laboratory values: CBC within normal limits; comprehensive metabolic panel: AST 28 U/L, ALT 31 U/L, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, LDH 151 U/L. TSH 2.8 mIU/L on levothyroxine 112 mcg. Urinalysis normal (no proteinuria to suggest nephritis). Nivolumab 480 mg every 4 weeks continued. Next imaging scheduled in 3 months.

At age 56 (approximately 21 months total on immunotherapy, 17 months on nivolumab maintenance), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast plus MRI brain for comprehensive restaging. Findings: Further deepening of response. Right cervical lymph node: 0.3 cm (no longer meets size criteria for measurable disease). Right axillary lymph node: 0.5 cm (decreased from 0.6 cm). Hepatic lesions: one residual lesion 0.3 cm (stable/decreasing); the second previously 0.4 cm lesion no longer visualized. No peritoneal disease. No new sites of involvement. LDH: 139 U/L. MRI brain: no intracranial metastases. Impression: Near-complete response by RECIST 1.1 criteria. No progressive disease. Continued durable benefit from nivolumab maintenance. Sum of target lesions approaching threshold for complete response on next scan.

At age 56 (approximately 24 months total on immunotherapy, 20 months on nivolumab maintenance), the patient had an oncologist office assessment and reached a major milestone: 2 years of total immunotherapy. Cancer: near-complete response on most recent imaging. ECOG performance status 0. No active immune-related adverse events. The patient feels well and has no melanoma-related symptoms. Discussion of treatment discontinuation at the 2-year mark, consistent with emerging data from long-term follow-up studies (CheckMate-067, KEYNOTE-006) suggesting that patients achieving deep responses may be able to stop treatment. The patient was counseled on relapse risk after discontinuation (estimated 30-40% at 2 years in historical studies) and the likely retreatability of relapse with PD-1 blockade. After thorough discussion including with a second opinion at a melanoma center of excellence, the patient opted to CONTINUE nivolumab for an additional 6 months given the residual 0.3 cm hepatic lesion and her preference to achieve complete response if possible before considering discontinuation. Plan: continue nivolumab 480 mg every 4 weeks, repeat PET-CT in 3 months to assess for complete metabolic response. Dermatology surveillance: clear. Thyroid: stable.

At age 56 (approximately 27 months total on immunotherapy, 23 months on nivolumab maintenance), the patient had a PET-CT (whole body FDG) for comprehensive restaging with attention to possible complete metabolic response. Findings: No FDG-avid lesions identified anywhere in the body. Previously noted residual hepatic lesion: 0.3 cm hypodense focus with no FDG uptake (SUVmax below background liver activity, effectively indeterminate/likely scar). Bilateral axillary regions: no FDG-avid adenopathy; nodes within normal size range. No FDG-avid peritoneal, subcutaneous, or nodal disease elsewhere. No brain metastases on concurrent MRI brain (gadolinium). LDH: 131 U/L (normal). Impression: Complete metabolic response by PET criteria. No evidence of active disease. The residual hepatic hypodensity is metabolically inactive and consistent with fibrotic scar rather than viable tumor. This represents a complete metabolic response after 27 months of immunotherapy.

At age 56 (27 months total on immunotherapy, 23 months on nivolumab maintenance), the patient had an oncologist office assessment following PET-CT demonstrating complete metabolic response. Cancer: no evidence of disease (complete metabolic response). ECOG performance status 0. This is a landmark visit. The patient has achieved a complete metabolic response to immunotherapy after initial progression on BRAF/MEK targeted therapy. She and her oncologist discussed treatment discontinuation. The decision was made to discontinue nivolumab after this visit (total 24 months of PD-1 monotherapy maintenance), consistent with the approach used in the CheckMate-067 5-year follow-up. The patient was counseled on the approximately 25-35% relapse risk after complete response and discontinuation, the likely manageability of relapse, and the critical importance of ongoing surveillance. Surveillance plan: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis every 3 months for 2 years, then every 6 months; MRI brain every 6 months for 2 years; dermatology every 4 months; laboratory monitoring including LDH quarterly. Endocrinology follow-up for hypothyroidism continued. Gastroenterology cleared patient from colitis follow-up. Last nivolumab infusion administered today. Patient expressed gratitude and cautious optimism.

documents.text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
INTERVAL RESTAGING — METASTATIC MELANOMA

CLINICAL INDICATION:
55-year-old female with Stage IV (M1c) metastatic melanoma, BRAF V600E-mutant, currently on dabra 150 mg twice daily plus tram 2 mg once daily. History of widespread cutaneous/subcutaneous nodules, bilateral axillary and inguinal lymphadenopathy, and multiple hepatic metastases at initial staging. Prior restaging CT at approximately 8 weeks demonstrated marked nodal and hepatic reduction. Now presented for approximately 6-month interval restaging to assess continued treatment response. Known history of Grade 2 febrile reaction to dabra, managed conservatively. History of hypothyroidism (clinically stable on levothyroxine).

COMPARISON:
Prior CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (approximately 8 weeks after treatment initiation) and baseline PET-CT at initial staging.

TECHNIQUE:
Helical CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis acquired following administration of 100 mL IV non-ionic iodinated contrast (Omnipaque 350). 5 mm axial sections with multiplanar reformats in coronal and sagittal planes. No oral contrast was administered. Radiation dose: DLP 812 mGy-cm.

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and airways: The lungs are clear bilaterally. No pulmonary nodules, consolidation, pleural effusion, or pneumothorax identified. The airways are patent to the subsegmental level. No mediastinal or hilar adenopathy by size criteria.

Cardiac and pericardium: The cardiac silhouette is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. No evidence of immune-related or drug-related cardiac involvement.

Chest wall and soft tissues: There are no residual cutaneous or subcutaneous nodules identified in the chest wall or upper back region. Previously noted cutaneous/subcutaneous nodules scattered over the trunk and bilateral upper extremities are no longer visualized. The dorsal trunk primary excision site shows expected post-surgical soft tissue changes, unchanged.

Axillary lymph nodes: Right axillary lymph node has decreased further to 0.8 cm in short axis (previously 1.1 cm at 8-week scan; 4.2 cm at baseline). The node retains a mildly rounded contour but demonstrates preserved fatty hilum. Left axillary lymph node: 0.4 cm, effectively within normal limits (previously 0.6 cm at 8 weeks; 1.8 cm at baseline). No new axillary adenopathy.

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size with smooth margins. Previously identified multifocal hepatic metastases show substantial and continued interval decrease. The dominant lesion in segment 6 now measures 0.6 cm in greatest diameter (previously 0.9 cm at 8-week scan; 2.4 cm at baseline), appearing as a small hypodense focus with ill-defined margins — this may represent residual viable tumor versus evolving necrosis/fibrosis and warrants continued surveillance. A lesion previously measuring 1.7 cm is no longer identifiable. A lesion previously measuring 1.4 cm is no longer identifiable. Of the 6 hepatic lesions documented at baseline, 4 are no longer visualized. Two residual hypodense foci remain: the segment 6 lesion at 0.6 cm (described above) and a second lesion in segment 5 measuring approximately 0.4 cm — this second lesion was not clearly measurable on prior study given its small size and is not confidently characterized on cross-sectional CT. No new hepatic lesions. No biliary ductal dilatation. Portal and hepatic venous patency appears maintained.

Spleen: Normal in size, homogeneous. No splenic lesions.

Pancreas: Unremarkable. No pancreatic ductal dilatation or focal lesion.

Adrenal glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are within normal limits. No adrenal lesions.

Kidneys: Both kidneys are normal in size with no hydronephrosis or focal cortical lesion. No renal calculi.

Gastrointestinal tract: Visualized portions of the stomach, small bowel, and colon appear unremarkable. No bowel wall thickening, obstruction, or pneumoperitoneum.

Retroperitoneum and mesentery: No retroperitoneal or mesenteric adenopathy. No ascites.

PELVIS:

Lymph nodes: Bilateral inguinal and pelvic lymph nodes are within normal limits. Previously noted bilateral inguinal adenopathy is no longer identified. Right inguinal region: no discrete pathological node (previously 2.1 cm at baseline, undetectable at 8-week scan, confirmed absent). Left inguinal region: no pathological adenopathy (previously 1.4 cm at baseline, undetectable at 8-week scan).

Pelvic organs and soft tissues: Uterus and adnexa are grossly unremarkable. No pelvic free fluid. No pelvic sidewall adenopathy. Bladder appears decompressed, unremarkable.

MUSCULOSKELETAL:
No lytic or blastic osseous lesions are identified. No pathological fractures. Visualized spine and pelvic bones appear intact.

SKIN AND SUBCUTANEOUS TISSUES:
As noted above, previously documented cutaneous and subcutaneous nodules scattered throughout the trunk and bilateral upper extremities are no longer visualized on this cross-sectional study. There are no new cutaneous or subcutaneous soft tissue masses in the imaged regions. Note is made of small well-healed scars over the left forearm and right shin consistent with the clinical history of prior excision procedures.

LABORATORY CORRELATION:
Serum LDH: 156 U/L (within normal limits per provided clinical data; markedly improved from 312 U/L at baseline and 187 U/L at prior restaging).

IMPRESSION:

1. Continued and deepening treatment response at approximately 6 months on dabra/tram. The overall picture is one of near-maximal response across all previously documented sites of disease.

2. Bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy markedly reduced — the right axillary node is now 0.8 cm (borderline by size criteria) and the left is 0.4 cm, effectively normal.

3. Bilateral inguinal adenopathy no longer demonstrated.

4. Hepatic disease substantially reduced: only two small residual hypodense foci remain (0.6 cm and 0.4 cm), with four of the original six lesions no longer visualized. The small residual hepatic foci are indeterminate at this size and could reflect minimal residual disease versus scarring/fibrosis; continued interval imaging is appropriate.

5. Previously widespread cutaneous and subcutaneous tumor deposits throughout the trunk and extremities are no longer visualized.

6. No new sites of disease identified.

7. LDH has normalized, consistent with favorable systemic disease trajectory.

Brain imaging not performed on this examination. Clinical team should ensure interval brain surveillance is current given the M1c staging and prior documentation of no intracranial disease.

Report reviewed and electronically signed.

CT CHEST / ABDOMEN / PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST
MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 55-year-old female with known metastatic melanoma (BRAF V600E+), Stage IV M1c. Currently on dabrafenib 150 mg BID plus trametinib 2 mg QD, initiated approximately 8 months ago. Patient presenting with new right-sided neck fullness, new palpable right cervical lymph node, and new right upper quadrant discomfort. Urgent restaging requested by oncology to evaluate for disease progression. Prior best response noted at 6-month imaging (CT from approximately 2 months prior).

TECHNIQUE:
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: Multidetector helical CT acquired from the thoracic inlet through the pubic symphysis following administration of 100 mL IV iohexol. Axial slices reconstructed at 2.5 mm with multiplanar reformations in coronal and sagittal planes. Lung windows reviewed for pulmonary parenchymal detail. Brain MRI performed on 1.5T system with pre- and post-gadolinium (0.1 mmol/kg Gadavist) sequences including axial T1, T2/FLAIR, DWI, and gadolinium-enhanced T1-weighted sequences.

She was initiated on dabrafenib 150 mg BID plus trametinib 2 mg daily in the first-line setting given her high tumor burden, BRAF V600E positivity, and desire for rapid disease control.

Her initial course on dab/tram was notable for a Grade 2 fever episode at approximately 6 weeks (dabrafenib briefly held, fully resolved) and development of two cutaneous squamous cell carcinomas on the left forearm and right shin at approximately 7 months (managed by dermatology with simple excision, margins clear). She achieved a marked early response at the 8-week restaging scan — right axillary node down from 4.2 cm to 1.1 cm, hepatic lesions substantially reduced — and continued to deepen her response through the 6-month restaging.

Unfortunately, at approximately 8 months, she began reporting new right-sided neck fullness and intermittent right upper quadrant discomfort. Notably, on her original Foundation One CDx panel, she harbored concurrent PTEN loss and an NF1 splice site mutation — a co-mutation pattern we discussed at the time of treatment initiation as carrying higher risk for earlier resistance, which has borne out clinically.

1. STAGE IV BRAF V600E-MUTANT METASTATIC MELANOMA — PROGRESSION ON FIRST-LINE DABRAFENIB/TRAMETINIB

Ms. Okonkwo has experienced clinical and radiographic progression after an approximately 8-month response to dabrafenib/trametinib, which is broadly consistent with the median PFS historically observed with this regimen. The acquired resistance profile on liquid biopsy — MEK1 C121S mutation and BRAF amplification — confirms MAPK pathway reactivation as the resistance mechanism. This is not retrievable with re-challenge of the same BRAF/MEK inhibitor combination.

We have had an extended discussion today about second-line treatment options. The strongest option for this patient is combination immunotherapy with ipilimumab (ipi) plus nivolumab (nivo) based on the CheckMate-067 data demonstrating a 5-year overall survival of approximately 52% with this combination in advanced melanoma. Key factors favoring ipi/nivo in her case:
   - High TMB: 28 mut/Mb (high, favors immunotherapy benefit)
   - PD-L1 TPS 15% (borderline, does not preclude benefit)
   - MSS status (no MSI-H to specifically enhance benefit beyond TMB)

She has been given detailed written instructions on when to call the clinic or go to the ED, particularly regarding diarrhea. HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Renata Okonkwo is a 56-year-old woman with a history of Stage IV M1c BRAF V600E-mutated melanoma, primary site dorsal trunk (T4b N3c M1c at presentation), who presents today for a landmark follow-up visit in the context of a recently confirmed complete metabolic response on whole-body FDG PET-CT.

To summarize the clinical course briefly: she was initially diagnosed with widely metastatic melanoma involving bilateral axillary and inguinal nodal basins, multiple cutaneous/subcutaneous deposits, and six hepatic metastases. She was treated first-line with dabrafenib (Tafinlar) 150 mg BID plus trametinib (Mekinist) 2 mg daily, achieving a rapid and dramatic partial response with LDH normalization. She experienced a single episode of Grade 2 dabrafenib-induced fever (managed with brief hold and acetaminophen) and two cutaneous SCCs on the left forearm and right shin (excised with clear margins), a class-effect of BRAF inhibition. At roughly 8 months, she progressed with new cervical adenopathy, worsening axillary disease, new hepatic lesions, and a small peritoneal nodule. Resistance profiling via ctDNA (Guardant360) and repeat biopsy of the right cervical node showed acquisition of a MAP2K1 C121S mutation and BRAF amplification, consistent with MAPK reactivation. Targeted therapy was discontinued.

She was then initiated on second-line combination immunotherapy with ipilimumab 3 mg/kg plus nivolumab (Opdivo) 1 mg/kg IV q3 weeks per the CheckMate-067 schema. After cycle 2 she developed a Grade 1-2 immune-related dermatitis, managed with topical triamcinolone. Following cycle 3, she was hospitalized for 7 days with Grade 3 immune-related colitis (8-10 watery stools/day, sigmoidoscopy-confirmed with crypt abscesses), requiring IV methylprednisolone followed by infliximab 5 mg/kg IV for steroid-refractory disease and an 8-week oral prednisone taper. Ipilimumab was permanently discontinued; nivolumab was held and eventually restarted after prednisone was tapered below 10 mg/day. She received 3 of the intended 4 induction cycles total.

Restaging at ~14 weeks from the start of ipi/nivo showed a partial response (~30% reduction). Nivolumab maintenance at 480 mg IV q4 weeks was resumed at approximately 16 weeks from initiation, with subsequent deepening of response on serial imaging. At ~6 months on maintenance, she had achieved a deep partial response with near-disappearance of hepatic metastases and resolution of peritoneal disease. By ~21 months of total immunotherapy, she met criteria for near-complete response, with only a 0.3 cm hepatic hypodensity remaining. 1. METASTATIC MELANOMA, BRAF V600E POSITIVE, STAGE IV M1c — COMPLETE METABOLIC RESPONSE
This is a landmark visit. I spent significant time reviewing this with the patient and her partner today.

Treatment discontinuation plan: We have discussed at length the decision to stop nivolumab today. At this juncture — 27 months from initiation of immunotherapy and 23 months on Opdivo maintenance — she has achieved a complete metabolic response by PET criteria, with the residual hepatic hypodensity appearing consistent with scar. The LDH is at its historical nadir (131 U/L). All measurable disease has resolved. Clinically, she is asymptomatic and ECOG 0.

The rationale for discontinuation is supported by the CheckMate-067 5-year follow-up data and the KEYNOTE-006 long-term data, which show that patients achieving complete responses on PD-1 or CTLA-4/PD-1 therapy with drug discontinuation can maintain durable remission in a meaningful proportion of cases. I discussed with the patient that the estimated risk of relapse following complete response and treatment discontinuation is in the range of 25-35% at 2-3 years in available historical series, and that relapse, if it occurs, is typically manageable with retreatment (either re-challenge with PD-1 therapy or alternative regimens). It was important for me to be transparent that long-term data for patients who stopped after CMR in the M1c setting are still maturing, and that this decision carries genuine uncertainty.

After a thorough discussion and the patient's own reflection following her second opinion at [melanoma center of excellence], she has elected to discontinue nivolumab today. Given M1c disease with prior hepatic and peritoneal involvement, we are implementing a close surveillance protocol:

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. METASTATIC MELANOMA, Stage IV M1c (BRAF V600E+) — COMPLETE METABOLIC RESPONSE

Ms. Okonkwo has achieved what is a remarkable outcome: a complete metabolic response following a protracted course of immunotherapy in the setting of M1c melanoma. Her clinical trajectory — initial BRAF/MEK therapy with rapid partial response, acquired resistance at 8 months, transition to ipi/nivo combination with Grade 3 colitis and three-quarter course completion, followed by 23 months of nivolumab maintenance with progressively deepening response — is illustrative of the complex management decisions that characterize this disease.

Treatment discontinuation discussion:
We had a detailed and thorough discussion today regarding discontinuation of nivolumab. We reviewed the available evidence from long-term follow-up of CheckMate-067 and other pivotal trials, acknowledging that prospective data on elective discontinuation after achieving complete response remain limited. Key points discussed:
- She has now received ~24 months of PD-1 monotherapy (23 months nivo maintenance + prior combination exposure)
- PET-CT confirms CMR — metabolically no evidence of disease
- Estimated relapse risk after complete response and discontinuation is in the range of 25-35% based on available retrospective and registry data, with the recognition that many relapses are re-treatable with PD-1 blockade
- Ongoing treatment carries continued, albeit lower than combination, risk of immune-related toxicity — her history of Grade 3 colitis and hypothyroidism are taken into account
- Quality of life and her personal preference are central to this decision

After thorough discussion, with input from a second opinion obtained at an NCI-designated melanoma center of excellence approximately 3 months ago, the patient has decided to DISCONTINUE nivolumab today. She received her last infusion of Opdivo 480 mg IV this visit. Total duration of PD-1 blockade: approximately 24 months (nivo maintenance); total immunotherapy exposure approximately 27 months.

This decision is supported by: CMR on PET, normal LDH, absence of any active irAEs, excellent functional status (ECOG 0), and patient preference after fully informed discussion.

- CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast every 3 months for the first 2 years off therapy
- MRI brain with gadolinium every 6 months for 2 years (given prior M1c disease and ongoing risk)
- LDH and comprehensive labs (CBC, CMP, LFTs, TSH) quarterly
- Dermatology every 4 months — ongoing risk of primary melanoma/new primary given history of multiple dysplastic nevi and prior BRAF-inhibitor-related SCCs
- Patient instructed to report immediately: new lumps, bumps, skin changes, neurological symptoms, GI symptoms (any return of diarrhea), shortness of breath, or visual changes

Well-controlled on levothyroxine 112 mcg daily. TSH 2.4 mIU/L today. Continue current dose. Endocrinology follow-up every 6 months. Levothyroxine continued regardless of nivolumab discontinuation, as checkpoint-induced hypothyroidism is generally permanent.

Gastroenterology has cleared Ms. Okonkwo from active colitis follow-up. She is no longer on any immunosuppressive therapy. If diarrhea recurs — even in the absence of further immunotherapy — GI re-evaluation warranted to distinguish late flare from other etiologies. She is aware.

5. PSYCHOSOCIAL / PATIENT EDUCATION:
Ms. Okonkwo expressed both gratitude and a degree of anxiety regarding stopping treatment that had been working. This is entirely expected and was validated. We reviewed the robust data supporting that the immunological memory established by checkpoint blockade can persist after drug discontinuation. She was referred to an oncology social worker for support during this transition. She has a robust support network and remains fully employed and active.

Return to clinic in 3 months (with repeat CT imaging at that time). Patient has my direct contact information and that of the on-call team for urgent concerns.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Okonkwo is a 56-year-old woman with Stage IV M1c BRAF V600E+ malignant melanoma who has achieved complete metabolic response after a prolonged course of immunotherapy. This is a remarkable outcome given her initial disease burden (M1c with hepatic, peritoneal, nodal, and subcutaneous involvement, LDH 1.5x ULN, high-risk genomic profile with PTEN loss, NF1 mutation, and TP53 mutation).

1. MELANOMA — COMPLETE METABOLIC RESPONSE, TREATMENT DISCONTINUATION:
After extensive discussion today that the patient had clearly thought through carefully in advance, I am recommending discontinuation of nivolumab. This is the appropriate decision at this juncture for the following reasons: (a) she has achieved PET-confirmed complete metabolic response, (b) she has now completed 24 months of PD-1 monotherapy maintenance (27 months total immunotherapy), and (c) continuing beyond confirmed complete response without additional evidence of benefit is not supported by the available data and carries ongoing cumulative toxicity risk. The data from CheckMate-067 long-term follow-up and from the KEYNOTE-006 experience suggest that patients who achieve complete response and discontinue treatment can have durable outcomes, though relapse remains possible in approximately 25-35% at 2 years. I reviewed the relapse risk with her in detail. She understands that if relapse occurs, re-induction with PD-1-based therapy or potentially combination immunotherapy is likely manageable, though retreatment after prior ipilimumab and colitis would require careful consideration (ipi re-challenge would be avoided given prior Grade 3 GI toxicity). She understands and accepts these considerations. Last nivolumab infusion administered today — Opdivo 480 mg IV over 30 minutes, without incident.

2. SURVEILLANCE PLAN:
   - CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast: every 3 months for 2 years, then every 6 months thereafter (per NCCN and institutional protocol for melanoma off treatment after complete response)
   - MRI brain with gadolinium: every 6 months for 2 years (continued brain surveillance given M1c disease history, even without prior brain mets)
   - Dermatology: every 4 months (increased frequency given history of cutaneous SCCs on BRAF inhibitor and ongoing melanoma surveillance)
   - LDH quarterly
   - CBC and CMP every 3 months for first year off treatment to monitor for delayed immune-related toxicities
   - TSH every 3-4 months (ongoing monitoring for hypothyroidism, which may require dose adjustment independent of immunotherapy)

3. IMMUNE-RELATED ADVERSE EVENT MONITORING (OFF TREATMENT):
Patients can develop immune-related adverse events even after immunotherapy discontinuation, sometimes weeks to months later. The patient is counseled to report any of the following immediately: diarrhea more than 3 stools above baseline per day, abdominal pain, skin rash, shortness of breath, cough, jaundice, vision changes, significant fatigue, or any new symptoms. She has a clear understanding of her prior colitis and the importance of early identification. Gastroenterology has cleared her from active follow-up (colitis in full sustained remission). She retains GI contact information in case of flare after discontinuation.

4. HYPOTHYROIDISM:
Continue levothyroxine 112 mcg daily. TSH was 2.8 mIU/L — well-controlled. Endocrinology follow-up ongoing. This will likely be a permanent condition regardless of immunotherapy status; immune-mediated thyroiditis following checkpoint inhibition rarely reverses. Patient is established with endocrinology.

5. PSYCHOSOCIAL:
Ms. Okonkwo is doing exceptionally well emotionally. She describes returning fully to her career as a landscape architect, engaging in her community, and maintaining healthy habits including regular walking and yoga. She tearfully expressed gratitude today. I reminded her that while we are celebrating an extraordinary result, the importance of surveillance cannot be overstated and she should call with any new symptoms or concerns without delay. I emphasized we will remain her medical home for long-term follow-up. She has support from her partner and family. Referral to cancer survivorship program discussed and patient interested — referral placed today.

6. RETURN PLAN:
First post-treatment surveillance visit in approximately 3 months with CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and lab work. MRI brain to be scheduled at 6 months post-discontinuation. Patient to contact clinic if any new symptoms arise in the interim.

METHODOLOGY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
This report was generated using the FoundationOne CDx (F1CDx) assay, a next-generation sequencing (NGS)-based in vitro diagnostic device cleared by the FDA as a companion diagnostic. The assay interrogates the full coding sequence of 324 cancer-relevant genes, select introns of 36 genes for rearrangement detection, whole genome copy number analysis (shallow), and two genomic signatures (tumor mutational burden [TMB] and microsatellite instability [MSI]). Additionally, select genes were assessed at higher depth for variant detection using a hybrid capture-based approach, with a mean exon coverage of >500x. TERT promoter (c.-124 and c.-146 positions) is assessed by a targeted deep sequencing addon. PD-L1 expression (22C3 companion diagnostic) was performed as a separate immunohistochemical assay on the same specimen block.

A separate germline analysis was performed on a concurrently submitted peripheral blood sample using a targeted hereditary cancer panel (BRCA1, BRCA2, and 68 additional cancer predisposition genes; Myriad myRisk). Results are summarized in the Germline section below.

DETAILED VARIANT INTERPRETATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

BRAF p.V600E (Tier I — Actionable)
The BRAF p.V600E substitution (valine-to-glutamate at codon 600) is the most common oncogenic mutation in cutaneous melanoma, occurring in approximately 50% of cases. This hotspot mutation results in constitutive kinase activation of the MAPK/ERK pathway independent of upstream RAS signaling. A variant allele frequency of 48% in this specimen is consistent with the estimated tumor purity and suggests a clonally dominant event. BRAF V600E is a companion diagnostic biomarker for FDA-approved BRAF inhibitors (vemurafenib, dabrafenib) and the dabrafenib/trametinib combination (Tafinlar/Mekinist), as well as encorafenib/binimetinib (Braftovi/Mektovi). This alteration is also identified on the FoundationOne CDx as a companion diagnostic for several approved agents.

CDKN2A p.Q95* / CDKN2B Homozygous Deletion (Tier II)
The CDKN2A gene at chromosome 9p21 encodes p16/INK4A, a critical negative regulator of CDK4/6 and the G1 cell-cycle checkpoint. The nonsense mutation p.Q95* results in premature termination and loss of p16 function. Co-deletion of CDKN2B (which encodes p15/INK4B) at allelic copy number of zero indicates homozygous deletion of the entire 9p21.3 locus, a pattern frequently seen in aggressive cutaneous melanoma. The concurrent CDKN2A nonsense mutation and CDKN2B co-deletion represent biallelic inactivation of the p16-CDK4/6-RB axis. This loss may confer sensitivity to CDK4/6 inhibitors (e.g., palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib) in combination strategies, though clinical utility in melanoma remains investigational. CDK4 copy number gain (see below) further deregulates the G1 checkpoint.

PTEN p.R130G (Tier II — Likely Pathogenic, Loss of Function)
PTEN encodes a lipid phosphatase that antagonizes the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling axis. The p.R130G missense substitution occurs at a critical catalytic residue within the phosphatase domain and is a recognized loss-of-function alteration. PTEN loss is found in approximately 20-30% of metastatic melanomas and is associated with constitutive AKT activation and resistance to MAPK pathway inhibition. The co-occurrence of PTEN loss with BRAF V600E in this specimen is noteworthy: pre-clinical and clinical data indicate that PTEN-null BRAF-mutant melanomas demonstrate reduced sensitivity to BRAF/MEK inhibitor combinations, with PI3K pathway activation serving as a bypass mechanism. A variant allele frequency of 31% suggests possible subclonal heterogeneity or hemizygous loss at the PTEN locus.

NF1 Splice Site c.1392-1G>A (Tier II — Likely Pathogenic)
NF1 encodes neurofibromin, a GTPase-activating protein (GAP) that negatively regulates RAS by accelerating GTP hydrolysis. The intronic splice acceptor mutation c.1392-1G>A is predicted to disrupt canonical splicing and result in exon skipping or intron retention, leading to a truncated or functionally impaired neurofibromin. NF1 loss-of-function results in hyperactivation of RAS and downstream MAPK and PI3K signaling. In the context of this tumor, the co-mutation of NF1 (RAS activator) and BRAF V600E (direct MAPK activator) alongside PTEN loss creates a highly activated oncogenic signaling milieu. NF1 mutations are found in approximately 14% of cutaneous melanomas as drivers (so-called "NF1-mutant" melanoma subtype) but when co-occurring with BRAF V600E, as here, are more likely representing secondary/co-mutational events. Notably, the relatively lower VAF (22%) may indicate subclonal representation, though germline NF1 has been excluded.

TP53 p.R248W (Tier II — Pathogenic, Gain of Function)
TP53 is the most commonly mutated tumor suppressor gene in human cancer. The p.R248W hotspot mutation at codon 248 (arginine-to-tryptophan) is one of the most frequently observed TP53 gain-of-function mutations. Unlike loss-of-function TP53 mutations, p.R248W produces a mutant p53 protein that actively suppresses the transcriptional activity of wild-type p53 family members (p63, p73) and gains novel oncogenic transcriptional activities, promoting invasion, chemoresistance, and genomic instability. A VAF of 39% suggests clonal representation consistent with tumor purity. TP53 gain-of-function mutations are associated with more aggressive disease behavior and genomic instability, consistent with the elevated mitotic rate and high TMB observed in this specimen.

TERT Promoter c.-124C>T (Tier II)
The TERT promoter mutation c.-124C>T (also described as C228T in the promoter) creates a de novo ETS transcription factor binding motif, resulting in markedly elevated TERT expression and telomerase activity. TERT promoter mutations are found in approximately 70-75% of cutaneous melanomas and represent an early and near-universal driver event in this malignancy, contributing to replicative immortality. A high VAF of 55% in this specimen is consistent with clonal, likely early acquisition. No direct therapeutic targeting of TERT is currently clinically validated.

MCL1 Amplification (Tier III)
MCL1, encoding an anti-apoptotic BCL-2 family protein, is amplified (copy number 8) in this tumor. MCL1 amplification provides a mechanism of resistance to apoptosis and has been associated with resistance to cytotoxic chemotherapy and targeted agents. MCL1 inhibitors (e.g., AMG 176, S64315/MIK665) are under early-phase clinical investigation. CDK4 copy number gain (CN 4) further deregulates the G1 checkpoint in the setting of complete CDKN2A/2B loss.

RAC1 p.P29S (Tier II — Likely Pathogenic)
RAC1 p.P29S is a recurrent hotspot mutation found in approximately 5-9% of sun-exposed cutaneous melanomas. RAC1 is a member of the Rho GTPase family; the P29S substitution creates a constitutively activated, fast-cycling GTPase that promotes actin polymerization, cell motility, cell cycle entry, and melanocyte proliferation. This mutation is associated with ultraviolet-signature mutational patterns and may contribute to resistance to BRAF-targeted therapy through MEK-independent activation of downstream pathways.

Co-mutation Pattern and Resistance Implications
The co-occurrence of BRAF V600E with concurrent PTEN loss (p.R130G), NF1 splice-site inactivation, and RAC1 P29S in this tumor represents a molecularly complex landscape with multiple potential mediators of intrinsic and early acquired resistance to BRAF/MEK inhibitor therapy. Published data from multiple institutional cohorts and the COMBI-v/COMBI-d resistance analyses identify PTEN loss as a key driver of PI3K/AKT-mediated bypass of MAPK inhibition. NF1 co-mutation can maintain RAS-GTP levels sufficient to re-engage CRAF-dependent MEK activation despite BRAF inhibitor presence. These findings carry important clinical implications: while initial response to BRAF/MEK inhibition is expected, depth and durability of response may be attenuated compared to BRAF V600E-only tumors. Close monitoring for early progression is warranted.

High TMB (28 mut/Mb) and prior PD-L1 positivity (TPS 15%) are favorable predictors of potential response to immune checkpoint blockade, which should be considered as the next therapeutic strategy should targeted therapy fail.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

DIAGNOSIS: Stage IV (M1c) metastatic melanoma, BRAF V600E-positive, arising from primary of the mid-back (skin of trunk). High-volume multiorgan disease with bilateral nodal, subcutaneous, and hepatic involvement. No brain metastases at this time. Performance status ECOG 1. Symptomatic from tender right axillary mass, pruritic cutaneous nodules, and moderate fatigue.

MS. OKONKWO presents with newly diagnosed high-burden Stage IV M1c melanoma. Several prognostic factors are worth noting: LDH is elevated at 1.5x ULN (associated with poorer outcomes in metastatic melanoma), she has hepatic involvement (M1c designation), ulceration and high mitotic rate in the primary, brisk TIL infiltrate (potentially favorable immunologically), and a complex molecular profile. The presence of a BRAF V600E hotspot mutation opens the door to oral targeted therapy with BRAF/MEK inhibition, which typically achieves rapid tumor regression and symptom relief, albeit with limited durability. Simultaneously, her high TMB (28 mut/Mb) and moderate PD-L1 expression (15%) make her a reasonable candidate for immunotherapy. The co-mutation pattern on genomic profiling is notable: concurrent PTEN loss and NF1 splice site mutation alongside BRAF V600E is a recognized profile associated with accelerated resistance to BRAF/MEK targeted therapy via PI3K/AKT pathway bypass signaling and RAS-independent MAPK reactivation. This was discussed with the patient.

TREATMENT DECISION:
Given (1) high-volume symptomatic disease with hepatic involvement requiring rapid disease control, (2) BRAF V600E positivity, (3) ECOG 1 with preserved organ function, and (4) patient preference for prompt initiation of treatment rather than enrollment in a clinical trial (clinical trial enrollment was discussed and declined by the patient, who wishes to begin therapy immediately):

RECOMMENDATION: Initiate BRAF/MEK inhibitor combination with dabrafenib (Tafinlar) 150 mg PO BID plus trametinib (Mekinist) 2 mg PO once daily. This regimen offers the most rapid disease control option and high objective response rate (~65–70% in BRAF-mutant unresectable/metastatic melanoma), which is appropriate given her symptomatic burden. The plan will be to reassess response at approximately 8 weeks with CT restaging, and to have a detailed discussion regarding the expected duration of response and the strategy for transition to immunotherapy at the time of progression.

IMPORTANT MOLECULAR CONSIDERATIONS: The PTEN loss, NF1 mutation, and TP53 gain-of-function co-mutations on FoundationOne CDx are consistent with a biologically aggressive phenotype. PTEN loss in particular is a recognized mediator of both primary and acquired resistance to BRAF/MEK inhibition. The patient was counseled that median duration of response to targeted therapy in the historical literature is approximately 10–14 months, and that progression is expected; transition to checkpoint immunotherapy (likely combination ipilimumab plus nivolumab given high TMB and moderate PD-L1) is being planned in advance. Clinical trial enrollment will be revisited at time of progression if appropriate options are available.

ADDITIONAL MANAGEMENT:
- Ophthalmology: Baseline ophthalmologic examination scheduled given patient's history of anterior uveitis and the theoretical concern for uveal toxicity with targeted agents; also important as a baseline prior to potential future immunotherapy
- Thyroid: Continue levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. TSH normal at this visit. Will continue to monitor — both BRAF inhibitors and, in the future, PD-1 blockade can affect thyroid function
- Dermatology: Baseline dermatology referral/surveillance established — BRAF inhibitor use is associated with paradoxical MAPK activation in BRAF wild-type keratinocytes, leading to squamoproliferative lesions and cutaneous SCCs; patient counseled and full-body skin surveillance every 8 weeks while on dabrafenib recommended
- Hepatic function monitoring: Baseline liver enzymes mildly elevated (AST 52, ALT 47, ALP 128) in the context of hepatic metastases; will recheck after first 2 weeks on therapy and monitor closely for drug-induced hepatotoxicity vs. disease-related changes

- Social work referral placed for support services and financial counseling regarding oral targeted therapy copay assistance programs
- Patient education: Extensive time spent reviewing diagnosis, prognosis, rationale for treatment sequencing, and immune-related adverse events that may be encountered with future immunotherapy. Patient and partner present; both expressed understanding. Written materials provided.

**HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS**
Ms. Renata Okonkwo is a 55-year-old female with a history of multiple atypical nevi since her 30s who presented several months ago with Stage IV M1c malignant melanoma arising from a dysplastic compound nevus of the mid-back (Breslow 5.8 mm, Clark level V, ulcerated, mitotic rate 8/mm2, lymphovascular invasion present). At initial staging she was found to have widespread disease: bilateral axillary and inguinal lymphadenopathy (largest right axillary node 4.2 cm with extranodal extension), multiple cutaneous/subcutaneous metastases on the trunk and upper extremities (up to 3.1 cm), and six hepatic metastases throughout both lobes (largest 2.4 cm in segment 6). LDH was elevated at 312 U/L at that time. NGS via FoundationOne CDx demonstrated BRAF V600E mutation (AF 48%), concurrent CDKN2A loss, PTEN p.R130G mutation, NF1 splice-site mutation, TP53 R248W, and TERT promoter mutation, with high TMB at 28 mut/Mb. PD-L1 TPS was 15%. Given the BRAF V600E positivity, high disease burden with hepatic involvement, and symptomatic disease (tender right axillary mass, pruritic cutaneous nodules, fatigue), the decision was made to initiate dabrafenib 150 mg twice daily plus trametinib 2 mg once daily for rapid disease control rather than proceeding to frontline immunotherapy.

She is now 8 weeks into therapy. She reports a dramatic symptomatic improvement. The tender right axillary mass is no longer palpable to her. The pruritic cutaneous nodules have resolved. Fatigue has substantially improved and she describes her energy level as "nearly back to normal." She has resumed many of her activities as a landscape architect and is back to light outdoor work. She had one episode of fever approximately 6 weeks in — temperature reached 39.4°C, with rigors and myalgias, lasting approximately 3 days. Dabrafenib was held for 3 days per protocol, acetaminophen and oral hydration were used, and naproxen was added empirically as prophylaxis against recurrent fever episodes. She restarted dabrafenib at full dose after resolution and has had no further febrile episodes. She takes both medications consistently in the morning and evening. No difficulty swallowing capsules. No rash, no peripheral edema, no visual changes, no headache. Bowel habits normal, 1-2 loose stools per day (baseline for her). No bleeding. Appetite good. She has not missed any doses since the 3-day hold.

Baseline ophthalmologic exam was performed given her prior history of anterior uveitis — ophthalmology noted no active uveitis and cleared her for continued BRAF/MEK inhibition with monitoring every 3 months. She continues levothyroxine for hypothyroidism.

She is here today to review the restaging CT results and discuss the path forward.

1. **Metastatic melanoma, BRAF V600E+, Stage IV M1c — marked partial response at 8 weeks on dabrafenib/trametinib**
   - This is a gratifying and expected early response to combination BRAF/MEK inhibition. Normalization of LDH from 312 → 187 U/L, resolution of palpable nodal disease and cutaneous deposits, and dramatic hepatic response are all highly favorable signs.

Historical data (COMBI-d, COMBI-v) suggest a median duration of response of roughly 10–14 months, though durability varies. We have discussed the co-mutation landscape from her FoundationOne CDx result: concurrent PTEN loss (p.R130G) and NF1 splice-site mutation are recognized molecular features associated with potentially earlier MAPK-pathway reactivation and acquired resistance. I counseled her on this clearly but without creating undue alarm — many patients do exceed the median, and her response depth at 8 weeks is encouraging.
   - We have discussed the plan for transition to immunotherapy at the time of progression. Given her high TMB (28 mut/Mb), PD-L1 15%, and the likely preservation of immunogenicity, we anticipate a meaningful chance of benefit from checkpoint inhibitor therapy when that time comes.

5. **Ophthalmology**

6. **General supportive care**
   - Adequate sun protection reinforced (SPF 50+, protective clothing) — particularly important given BRAF inhibitor therapy and baseline history of multiple atypical nevi.

   - Patient is well-nourished, weight stable. No nutritional supplementation needed.

**Return to clinic:** 4 weeks for routine toxicity check and labs; full restaging CT at the 6-month mark from treatment initiation. Patient has my direct nurse line and is instructed to call for fever >38.5°C, any new lumps, significant GI symptoms, visual changes, or other concerning symptoms.
